summary: Phase-2 Regelchunk: saving_throws aus TSR Inc - AD&D 2nd Edition - PHBR02 - The Complete Thief's Handbook.pdf.

source_text.text:
[PDF_PAGE 114 | score=17] Poison Gases are rare, expensive, and highly dangerous. A typical dose is a clay x glass jar weighing 5 pounds. If the jar is shattered or opened, gas fills a 10' x 10' x 10' cube. It disperses after one round un- less contained on all sides. The gas lasts :or 2d6 turns in an enclosed room; 2d6 rounds in a room with at least one open door, window, arrow slit, etc. It might be possible for creative play- :rs to create larger doses of poison gas, >ut such lethal devices are left to the DMs discretion. Poison gases generally have the ef- 'ects of poison type D, taking effect af- :er one or two minutes, inflicting 30 9oints of damage (2d6 with a successful ;ave). The damage is inflicted each tur
[PDF_PAGE 100 | score=9] Wax Pad This is a small dock of wax usually kept in a rigid :ontainer to keep it in one piece-the quivalent of a large snuff-box will do qicely. The thief uses the wax to record m impression of a key he wants to have iuplicated later. Obviously, the thief :an only do this if he has access to a tey, at least for a short time, and if he doesn’t wish to take a chance on bor- rowing the key for a while. From the rvax impression, the thief can have a lbcksmith make a duplicate of the origi- tal key, or attempt to make one himself ising a keymaking set, if he has one. With very soft wax, which may need ieat-softening on the spot, the thief nay even be able to make a wax im- wession of such items a
[PDF_PAGE 27 | score=8] Thief Kits I let alone prosper in his ways. Adventurers may be part of a thieves’ guild for easy access to equip- ment and training. They tend to be in- dependent, however, and dislike guilds that have demands beyond a simple membership fee. Secondary Skills: Any. Weapon Proficiencies: Any. Nonweapon Proficiencies: Required: None. Recommended: Player’s choice; among those that may be selected are Alertness, Boating, Fast-talking, Infor- mation Gathering, and Looting. Skill Progression: Adventurer thieves tend to spread their skill im- provements as evenly as possible, to al- low them to deal with the many different challenges the adventuring life presents. If there is any concentration, it i
[PDF_PAGE 101 | score=6] as far as half a mile if they are down- wind of it. I Dog Pepper' This may be dropped on the floor, like aniseed, to put dogs off the scent when pursuing a thief. It is less effective than aniseed, however, the pursuing dog being al- lowed a saving throw vs. poison to avoid the effect of the dog pepper abso- lutely. If the save is failed, though, the dog is unable to continue pursuit for ld4 + 1 turns. Dog pepper may more usefully be scattered into the air, a small packet holding enough to fill a 10-foot cube. The pepper will hang in the air for ld4 rounds after being flung into it. A dog entering the area during this time gets no saving throw to avoid the effect of the pepper. If dog pepper
[PDF_PAGE 28 | score=5] ough at half strength (i.e., no effect if e saving throw is successful, and if it's t, normal save damage is applied-see e Dungeon Master's Guide, p. 73). Taste is a fairly reliable, if dangerous, ethod of identifying a poison. It car- s a -5% penalty. After dabbing a ny bit on his tongue, the Assassin spits out. There is still a chance that the oison will affect the Assassin: 25% for jected poison, 75% for ingested, and 0% for contact. The poison's effects, any, are half strength (see above). The most certain way of identifying a son is by its symptom (no penalty on attempt). The drawback of this thod is of course that you need a poi- ned character to examine. n Assassin with herbalism prof
[PDF_PAGE 87 | score=5] I Thie~ves’ Guilds possesses as a group, stocks of specialist thief equipment, and ledgers and ac- counts of the guild (membership dues and the like). These are all retained in his bags of holding. Marmel is not an expert fence, although he may use his Appraising proficiency to purchase and check items for the guild. Marmel began life as an apprentice to a Furyondian mage, and had to suffer ill-treatment as the mage developed an insidious form of insanity. His master refused to allow Marmel to make his own way in the world, but the unfortu- nate apprentice got his chance when the mage was forced to accompany a Fu- ryondian naval foray against some of the declining piratical force of the Sea 
[PDF_PAGE 113 | score=5] A simpler way to make a room dr chest more secure simply to protect it lock. Iron-bound three built-in locks were not unco on in rich treasure vaults. Certainly a powerful and suspi- cious wizard w uld not entrust his spellbooks to a t c , binet with only a sin- gle lock. A thief attempt- anyone within ezirshot. If a thief fails a ilent attempt, he can- not try to open t e same lock normally. Animal Ass I stants ' A thief with an y at animal handling monkeys. Othed types can be used given the approv' 1 of the DM; develop and define the abiities of the animal us- these creatures. ' of the animal is or less to train the ferret for the task. The ferret, consequently, needs a roll of 17 or less
